title: Tiger bread
subtitle: White loaf with a crisp, cracked sesame crust
excerpt: Tiger bread is a white tin loaf finished with a rice-paste glaze that cracks during baking, giving the top a mottled, scaly pattern and a very crisp surface over a soft crumb. The glaze is mixed before baking and spread over the shaped dough before it goes into the oven.
sourceNotes: Tiger bread (Dutch: tijgerbrood) originated in the Netherlands. The distinctive rice-paste crust is a Dutch baking tradition, documented in Dutch baking manuals from the mid-twentieth century. The version common in Britain follows the same paste formula: rice flour, sesame oil, dried yeast, water, and a little sugar.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze. The crust softens on thawing but crisps again after 5 minutes in a warm oven.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The rice paste can be made up to an hour ahead; keep covered.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; drop to 200°C after 15 minutes
baking.steamMethod: none; the rice paste creates its own crust

content.text: Tiger bread is a Dutch tin loaf finished with a rice-flour paste that cracks as it bakes. The result is a very crisp, shatteringly thin top crust in a distinctive mottled pattern, over a soft, pillowy crumb. The paste is the whole trick; the bread itself is a standard white tin loaf.

Combine flour, yeast, salt, and oil in a large bowl. Add the lukewarm water and mix to a shaggy dough. Knead for 10 minutes by hand until smooth and elastic. A 65% hydration dough is soft and slightly tacky; it should clear the bowl but not fight you.
Cover and leave to rise at room temperature for about 75 minutes, until roughly doubled.
Knock back the dough and shape it into a log to fit a greased 900 g loaf tin. Cover and prove for 45 minutes until the dough crowns well above the rim.

Mix the rice flour, yeast, sesame oil, sugar, salt, and lukewarm water into a thick, pourable paste. Leave it for 15 minutes; it will start to bubble gently as the yeast activates. The consistency should coat the back of a spoon thickly. Make this while the loaf proofs.

Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Spread the tiger paste generously over the top of the proved loaf in an even layer. Bake for 15 minutes, then drop the heat to 200°C and bake for a further 18 to 20 minutes. The loaf is done when the paste has cracked into a deep golden, scaly pattern and the loaf sounds hollow when tapped underneath. Cool fully on a wire rack before slicing; the cracking pattern firms as it cools.
